paragraphs: 颐和园 / The Summer Palace
开场白：颐和园 / Intro: The Summer Palace
咱们眼前这片被昆明湖波光映照着、万寿山绿意环绕的园林，就是中国现存规模最大、保存最完整的皇家园林——北京颐和园！接下来15分钟，咱们从东宫门开始，顺着"宫廷区→万寿山前山"的路线逛。咱们这就出发！

Good morning, everyone, and welcome! I'm Miya, your guide today, and I'm thrilled to have you along. The garden stretching out before us—Kunming Lake shimmering in the foreground, Longevity Hill rising green behind it—is the Summer Palace, the largest and best-preserved imperial garden in all of China. Over the next 15 minutes or so, we'll start at the East Palace Gate and follow the route of "Palace Area to Front Hill." We'll peek into the private quarters of Empress Dowager Cixi and Emperor Guangxu, stroll along the world's longest painted corridor, and climb up to the Tower of Buddhist Incense, the iconic centerpiece of the whole garden. Ready? Let's go!
一、颐和园的历史沿革 / I. Historical Evolution
辽代叫"金山行宫"；金代改名"玉泉山行宫"；元代叫"瓮山泊"；明代改成"好山园"；清代1750年乾隆建了"清漪园"；1860年英法联军烧毁；1888年慈禧挪用海军经费重建，改名"颐和园"。

Before we start walking, let me give you a quick timeline. This place has over a thousand years of history. Back in the Liao Dynasty (10th–12th century), it was a modest imperial summer retreat called "Golden Hill Palace." The Jin Dynasty renamed it "Yuquan Mountain Palace" and added some pavilions and ponds. Under the Yuan Dynasty, it became known as "Wengshan Lake"—Wengshan being the old name for Longevity Hill—and the famous engineer Guo Shoujing built irrigation works here. The Ming Dynasty emperor Xiaozong gave it the more poetic name "Haoshan Garden." But the real golden age came under the Qing Dynasty. In 1750, Emperor Qianlong commissioned "Qingyi Garden" as a birthday gift for his mother, and that's essentially the layout you see today. Tragically, in 1860, the Anglo-French forces burned it to the ground during the Second Opium War. Then, in 1888, Empress Dowager Cixi famously diverted naval funds to rebuild it and gave it the name we use today: the Summer Palace.
二、整体布局 / II. Overall Layout
颐和园占地约290万平方米，相当于4个故宫！北边靠万寿山，南边临昆明湖。整个园林分三大区：宫廷区、万寿山前山区、昆明湖区。

The Summer Palace covers roughly 2.9 million square meters—about four times the size of the Forbidden City. It's laid out with Longevity Hill to the north and Kunming Lake to the south, and the whole thing is divided into three main zones: the Palace Area near the East Palace Gate, the Front Hill of Longevity Hill where the iconic buildings are, and the Kunming Lake Area with its boats and bridges.
三、宫廷区 / III. Palace Area
1. 涵虚罨秀坊与东宫门 / Hanxu Yanxiu Archway & East Palace Gate
眼前这座牌坊就是"涵虚罨秀坊"，穿过就是东宫门，门楣上"颐和园"三个大字是光绪亲笔。

Right in front of us is the Hanxu Yanxiu Archway—a beautifully painted gateway that serves as the official entrance to the garden. "Hanxu" means "embracing the mist of heaven," and "Yanxiu" means "concealing beautiful scenery." Just past it is the East Palace Gate, the main entrance. Look up at the lintel—those three characters, "Yi He Yuan," were written by Emperor Guangxu himself.
2. 仁寿殿 / Hall of Benevolent Longevity
仁寿殿是颐和园最大的宫殿，相当于故宫太和殿。"仁寿"取自《论语》"仁者寿"。殿外铜凤在左、铜龙在右，"凤压龙"是慈禧权力的无声宣言。

Straight ahead is the Hall of Benevolent Longevity, the largest building in the Summer Palace and the emperor's equivalent of the Hall of Supreme Harmony in the Forbidden City. The name comes from Confucius's Analects—"the benevolent enjoy longevity." Now, here's a fun detail: look at the bronze dragon and phoenix flanking the entrance. In most imperial buildings, the dragon (representing the emperor) goes on the left. But here, the phoenix is on the left and the dragon is on the right. Why? Because Cixi was the one actually running the country behind the scenes. That "phoenix over dragon" arrangement was her not-so-subtle way of declaring who was really in charge.
3. 玉澜堂 / Hall of Jade Ripples
玉澜堂是光绪的寝宫兼书房。1898年戊戌变法失败后，慈禧把光绪软禁在这儿，走廊被砌上砖墙，直到去世都没踏出半步。

Just north of the main hall is the Hall of Jade Ripples, Emperor Guangxu's private quarters and study. The name evokes "jade-like ripples on Kunming Lake." It sounds lovely, but the history here is heavy. After the failed Hundred Days' Reform in 1898, Cixi placed Guangxu under house arrest in this very building. She had brick walls built across the surrounding corridors, leaving only one small door for entry and exit. Guangxu never set foot outside again until his death. If you look carefully, you can still see the traces of those prison walls.
4. 宜芸馆 / Yiyun Hall
宜芸馆是隆裕皇后的居所，装饰特别素净，低调得多。

Next to Jade Ripples is Yiyun Hall, the residence of Empress Longyu, Guangxu's wife. "Yiyun" means "a place suited for reading and scholarship." Compared with Cixi's lavish quarters, the decoration here is remarkably understated—light-colored wood furniture, simple blue curtains, minimal ornamentation. It tells you a lot about where Longyu stood in the palace pecking order.
5. 乐寿堂 / Hall of Joyful Longevity
乐寿堂是慈禧的"主卧"。堂前有太湖石"青芝岫"，堂内有西洋电灯、电话机，还有雕着"百鸟朝凤"的紫檀木柜。

And finally, the Hall of Joyful Longevity—Cixi's personal favorite. She spent her summers here escaping the heat, her winters basking in the sun, and she even took most of her meals in this building. The name comes from an ancient saying: "the wise find joy, the benevolent find longevity." Out front is a massive Taihu limestone boulder called "Qingzhi Xiu," which Emperor Qianlong had hauled all the way from Fangshan. The story goes that it took thousands of workers, hundreds of mules and horses, and several years just to move it—the transportation cost alone could have fed ten thousand families for a year. Inside, the furnishings are jaw-dropping: Western-style electric lights and a telephone (cutting-edge technology for late Qing China), and an enormous red sandalwood wardrobe intricately carved with a "hundred birds paying homage to the phoenix" motif.
四、万寿山前山 / IV. Front Hill of Longevity Hill
1. 长廊 / Long Corridor
长廊全长728米，273间，1992年列入吉尼斯世界纪录。廊顶彩绘14000多幅，没有一幅重样的！

Now let's walk along the world's longest corridor—the Long Corridor. It stretches 728 meters from the Moon-Inviting Gate in the east to the Stone Zhang Pavilion in the west, with 273 bays in total. It earned a spot in the Guinness World Records in 1992. But the real marvel isn't the length—it's the ceiling. Every single bay is decorated with a unique painted scene on its beams. There are over 14,000 paintings in all, and no two are alike. You'll find scenes from Journey to the West and Romance of the Three Kingdoms, landscapes of southern China, and all sorts of classical motifs. Strolling through here on a summer day, with lake breezes coming through the pillars, is pure pleasure.
2. 前山中轴线建筑 / Central Axis Buildings
从下往上：排云殿（慈禧祝寿的宴会厅）、佛香阁（高41米，颐和园标志）、智慧海（无梁殿，1008尊琉璃佛）。

North of the corridor, the central axis buildings climb the hillside like a grand staircase. At the base is the Hall of Dispelling Clouds, the core ceremonial hall where Cixi held her extravagant birthday banquets. Above it stands the Tower of Buddhist Incense—the undisputed landmark of the Summer Palace. It rises 41 meters (about 14 stories) on a 21-meter stone platform, and the views from the top are breathtaking. Originally it housed a magnificent thousand-armed Guanyin statue, which was looted during the 1860 invasion. And at the very top sits the Sea of Wisdom, a "beamless hall" constructed entirely from brick and stone—no wooden beams at all. Its walls are covered with 1,008 small glazed Buddha statues in yellow, green, and blue. Because it had no wood to burn, it was one of the few structures to survive the 1860 destruction.
五、世界荣誉 / V. World Honor
1998年，颐和园被列入《世界遗产名录》。
In 1998, the Summer Palace was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List, hailed as a masterpiece of Chinese classical garden art that embodies the ideal harmony between humanity and nature.
欢送词 / Farewell Speech
各位朋友，颐和园之旅到这儿就结束啦！希望今天的讲解能让大家记住颐和园的碧波、红墙和彩绘。祝大家旅途顺利，再见！

Well everyone, that brings our Summer Palace tour to a close! We've gone from the political intrigue of the Palace Area to the architectural splendor of the Front Hill. I hope today gave you a real appreciation for this place—every pavilion, every painting, every stone has a story. Thanks for spending the time with me, and enjoy the rest of your trip!
